title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Glucocorticoid Therapy
content: Deflazacort, an oxazoline derivative of prednisone, is sometimes preferred over prednisone as it has a better side effect profile and has an estimated dosage equivalency of 1:1.3 compared with prednisone. The recommended dosage is 0.9 mg/kg/day.

title: Becker Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Therapy of Muscle Dystrophy
content: There is no approved BMD treatment to date. However, the management approach to this condition is often similar to that for milder DMD cases. Corticosteroids have been the mainstay of treatment for patients with significant muscle weakness. Prednisolone at a dose of 0.75 mg/kg/day or deflazacort at a dose of 0.9 mg/kg/day may be given initially. [26] [27]

title: Becker Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Therapy of Muscle Dystrophy
content: Corticosteroids should be started before physical disability develops and must continue even after losing ambulation. Glucocorticoid treatment helps prolong survival by delaying the onset of cardiomyopathy, improving pulmonary function, retarding scoliosis, and reducing the need for spinal surgery. [28] [29] The steroid dose should be reduced by 25% to 33% if adverse effects develop. Nitric oxide is occasionally prescribed to improve muscular circulation.

title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmaceutical Interventions
content: Steroids, specifically glucocorticoids like prednisone, administered at a dose of 0.75 mg/kg per day, significantly slow the progression of muscular dystrophy for up to 3 years. [189] While some patients may not tolerate glucocorticoid therapy due to factors like weight gain and an increased risk of fractures, recent evidence suggests that early oral steroid use can lead to markedly improved outcomes. [190] [191] This includes additional walking years for children and increased life expectancy. [71] The use of glucocorticoids in Becker dystrophy and their effectiveness in treating myotonic muscular dystrophy remain areas where research is ongoing, and evidence is not yet conclusive. [192] [193] Some individuals with facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy improve with steroid therapy, particularly when rapidly progressive weakness is observed. [194]

title: Dystrophinopathies -- Treatment / Management -- Cardiomyopathy
content: Corticosteroids have been proved to improve muscle strength and function. This is, therefore, the main therapy for children younger than 15 years of age, although not recommended for children below age 2. [15] Therapy can start with either prednisone (0.75 mg/kg per day, maximum 40 mg per day) or deflazacort (0.9 mg/kg per day, maximum 36 mg per day) when motor skills begin to decline. To assess efficacy, physicians can perform pulmonary function tests and timed muscle function tests, while also monitoring for side effects Cushing syndrome, short-stature, changes in behavior, gastrointestinal (GI)  symptoms, and osteopenia (increased risk for vertebral or long bone fractures [16] ). Should a severe side effect present, particularly excessive weight gain, doses can be decreased by 25% until reaching 50% of the original dose. There are conflicting views on the use of corticosteroids on BMD patients, as data supporting benefits are quite limited.

title: Rhabdomyolysis -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
content: Gene therapy is being explored for treating inherited muscular dystrophies such as Duchenne and Becker. This includes various technologies such as read-through of early stop codons and exon skipping. Edasalonexent (CAT-1004), a nuclear factor κB (NF-κB) inhibitor that combines structural elements of salicylic acid and docosahexaenoic acid, has been evaluated in a phase 3, placebo-controlled clinical trial with some reported success. ( NCT03703882 ). Vamorolone is a glucocorticoid that stabilizes membranes by reducing lipid peroxidation and inhibiting NF-kB-mediated inflammation; it has also demonstrated functional improvement of symptoms. Many other novel treatments are being studied. [20] [64]

title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Unfortunately, Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) is not only one of the most severe forms of inherited muscular dystrophies but also the most common hereditary neuromuscular disease. Sadly, there is no known treatment modality that halts the progression of the disease; available treatment options are palliative. Affected patients usually die in their twenties due to respiratory muscle weakness or cardiomyopathy. Current therapy is centered on treatment with glucocorticoids and physiotherapy to prevent orthopedic complications. This activity illustrates the pathophysiology and current management options for DMD and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for affected patients and families.

title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Introduction
content: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) is one of the most severe forms of inherited muscular dystrophies. It is the most common hereditary neuromuscular disease and does not exhibit a predilection for any race or ethnic group. Mutations in the dystrophin gene lead to progressive muscle fiber degeneration and weakness. This weakness may present initially with difficulty in ambulation but progressively advances to such an extent that affected patients are unable to carry out activities of daily living and must use wheelchairs. Cardiac and orthopedic complications are common, and death usually occurs in the twenties due to respiratory muscle weakness or cardiomyopathy. Current therapy is centered on treatment with glucocorticoids and physiotherapy to prevent orthopedic complications. [1] [2] [3]

title: Dermatomyositis -- Treatment / Management
content: The first-line treatment of muscle disease in dermatomyositis is systemic glucocorticoids with or without immunosuppressants. Although there is no standard systemic steroid regimen specified for dermatomyositis, the general principles of therapy are the same. Initially, prednisolone is given at high doses for the first few months until the muscle enzyme levels decline, and muscle strength improves. During this time, patients should be regularly evaluated for an adequate response, keeping in mind that it takes approximately six weeks for muscle enzymes to normalize. Also, it may require as long as three months for muscle weakness to improve. Once an adequate response occurs, the administration of systemic steroids is gradually tapered off over time. The total duration of therapy with systemic steroids usually spans between nine and twelve months. It is important to note that administering high dose glucocorticoids for more than six weeks may lead to glucocorticoid myopathy.

title: Becker Muscular Dystrophy -- Differential Diagnosis
content: DMD: more severe and earlier onset than BMD. The patient becomes wheelchair-bound earlier, and the length of survival is shorter. Patients usually have much lower dystrophin concentrations. [34]

title: Becker Muscular Dystrophy -- Prognosis
content: BMD generally has a milder clinical course than DMD. However, the chances of survival decrease with time as the disease progresses. Patients become dependent on supportive interventions to prolong life. The average life expectancy of individuals with BMD is about 40 to 50 years. Death is most commonly due to dilated cardiomyopathy. [42]

title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Cardiomyopathy
content: Surveillance consists of a cardiology assessment with ECG and echocardiogram. This should be performed at the time of diagnosis or by the age of 6 years. Routine surveillance should be performed once every two years until the age of 10 and then yearly after that. If evidence of cardiomyopathy is present, surveillance every six months is indicated.

content: Triamcinolone is used to treat multiple sclerosis and تَشَنُّج قَصَبِيّ, but it is contraindicated in حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة. It belongs to both the “Corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use” group and the “Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease” group, indicating its availability in nasal and inhaled formulations for airway inflammation. The therapeutic class “Adrenergic inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics” includes fixed‑dose combinations such as albuterol + beclomethasone, budesonide / formoterol, formoterol / mometasone (Momesalic), and vilanterol + fluticasone furoate; in these products budesonide, mometasone (Momesalic), and fluticasone furoate serve as the corticosteroid components. The “Corticosteroid nasal preparations for topical use” group comprises Momesalic, budesonid, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate, while the “Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease” group contains Momesalic, budesonid, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate, together with related steroid derivatives dexamethasone sodium phosphate, hydrocortisone sodium succinate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate as child agents. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate is specifically employed in the management of edematous conditions (حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة) and circulatory collapse (الانهيار في الدورة الدموية). Collectively, these agents and combinations provide a spectrum of nasal, inhaled, and systemic glucocorticoid therapies for inflammatory respiratory diseases, systemic edema, hemodynamic instability, and other inflammatory conditions, with defined indications and contraindications that guide patient‑centered care.

title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 504
content: Triamcinolone is a member of the class of moderately potent corticosteroids, which in turn is a subclass of the broader “corticosteroids, other combinations” group. As a moderately potent corticosteroid, triamcinolone may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي), but it is contraindicated in patients with fibrotic conditions (حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة). Weak corticosteroids are also classified under the “corticosteroids, other combinations” hierarchy, illustrating the spectrum of potency within this drug class. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, another corticosteroid formulation, is indicated for the management of fibrotic conditions (حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة) and for emergency treatment of circulatory collapse (الانهيار في الدورة الدموية). Together, these relationships delineate the therapeutic applications, potency classifications, and specific contraindications of corticosteroid agents in various clinical scenarios.

title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 447
content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease constitute a therapeutic class that includes ciclesonide and flunisolide as member agents. Within this class, several related glucocorticoid compounds are identified as child entities, namely dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate. These inhaled glucocorticoids are employed to reduce inflammation and improve airflow in patients with obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. In addition to their inhaled applications, dexamethasone acetate has been recognized for its potential role in the management of severe systemic inflammation, exemplified by its use in the treatment of septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة). Together, these agents illustrate the breadth of glucocorticoid therapy, ranging from targeted inhalation for airway disease to broader systemic anti‑inflammatory interventions.

Within this class, dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate are identified as specific child compounds. Each of these glucocorticoid formulations exerts potent anti‑inflammatory effects in the airways, helping to reduce bronchial hyper‑responsiveness, edema, and mucus production. title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
content: Triamcinolone is a corticosteroid parent compound that gives rise to multiple therapeutic subclasses, including systemic glucocorticoids, dermatologic preparations, ophthalmologic anti‑inflammatory agents, nasal sprays, inhaled glucocorticoids, and topical formulations for hemorrhoids and anal fissures. It is contraindicated in patients with growth‑related conditions, requiring caution in pediatric prescribing. Amcinonide, a potent (group III) corticosteroid derived from the triamcinolone lineage, exemplifies a plain dermatologic preparation within the Corticosteroids → Dermatological Preparations hierarchy, which also includes weak (group I), very potent (group IV), and combination products with antibiotics or other agents. title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2153
content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease and Glucocorticoids, Systemic represent two related categories of glucocorticoid therapy that share anti‑inflammatory mechanisms but differ in route of administration, with the former delivered by inhalation to treat airway obstruction and the latter administered systemically. Prednison (prednisone) is a specific systemic glucocorticoid medication and therefore belongs to the Glucocorticoids, Systemic class. Although classification schemes may list inhaled glucocorticoids as a subcategory within the broader glucocorticoid family, prednison is not an inhaled agent but a systemic drug. The relationship between Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease and Glucocorticoids, Systemic highlights their common pharmacologic basis while emphasizing the clinical distinction between inhaled and systemic delivery for optimal patient management.
